## Overview
Larry Heck is a senior researcher based at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, affiliated with the College of Computing, where he leads a research group focused on *building robust, scalable conversational and spoken-language systems*. His work sits at the intersection of **speech and language processing**, **deep learning**, and *interactive AI* — with enduring contributions to <u>dialogue systems</u>, <u>speaker recognition</u>, and <u>semantic modeling for retrieval and understanding</u>. Across industry and academia he has combined signal- and model-based approaches with modern neural architectures to tackle real-world conversational problems.

---

## Research Areas
Heck's research spans a broad set of topics in spoken and conversational AI informed by both early signal-processing work and contemporary deep learning. Core themes include neural semantic modeling for search (notably deep structured semantic models), sequence modeling for *slot filling* and intent classification using recurrent architectures, and hierarchical/contextual sequence models (e.g., CLSTM) for large-scale NLP. He has extensive work on speech-centric problems — from prosody and pitch modeling for speaker recognition to toolkits for speaker-ID research — and on practical, scalable components for dialogue systems: session and context modeling, multi-domain dialogue state tracking, unsupervised/zero-shot semantic parsing and domain adaptation, and methods for bootstrapping task-oriented agents (e.g., dialogue self-play / M2M). More recently his work also addresses model robustness and grounding: methods for visual grounding in vision+language models (HINT) and *incremental learning* techniques for updating deployed models without catastrophic forgetting.

---

## Notable Works
- "Learning deep structured semantic models for web search using clickthrough data" (2013) — Introduced large-scale neural approaches that project queries and documents into a common semantic space (DSSM), substantially advancing neural retrieval and influencing later neural ranking and representation work.
- "Using recurrent neural networks for slot filling in spoken language understanding" (2014) — Pioneered RNN architectures tailored to the slot-filling problem in SLU, improving sequence labeling for intent/argument extraction in conversational systems.
- "Building a conversational agent overnight with dialogue self-play" (2018) — Presented the M2M framework that combines automation and crowdsourcing to rapidly bootstrap goal-oriented dialogue agents, enabling scalable domain transfer and fast dialog agent development.

---

## Academic Background
Larry Heck's career bridges long-standing industrial research and academic leadership: prior to his current affiliation at Georgia Tech he contributed extensively within industry research labs (notably Microsoft Research), producing influential patents, toolkits (e.g., the MSR Identity Toolbox), and a large body of peer-reviewed publications. His publication record covers signal processing, HMM-based monitoring, prosody and pitch modeling for speaker recognition (1990s–2000s), and a sustained transition into deep learning for language, retrieval, and dialogue (2010s–2020s). His work has been widely adopted and cited (total citations: **15,328**), reflecting both methodological impact and practical systems deployment. He has held leadership and collaborative roles across interdisciplinary teams, mentoring students and researchers and contributing to the transition of research prototypes into production-capable conversational technologies.

4G7pc2IAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Greg Eisenhauer is a senior researcher and faculty member in the **College of Computing** at **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group focused on high-performance system software and data-intensive computing (see his homepage: http://www.cc.gatech.edu/~eisen). His work spans the design and implementation of scalable middleware, runtime systems, and tools that enable *online and interactive analysis* of large simulations and live service infrastructures. Across decades of work he has advanced <u>online monitoring and steering</u>, <u>scalable data staging and I/O</u>, and <u>flexible communication and streaming</u> abstractions that bridge traditional enterprise information flows and extreme-scale scientific computing.

---

## Research Areas
Eisenhauer’s research centers on the systems and middleware required to make modern high-performance and data-intensive applications usable and efficient. He has repeatedly addressed the tension between computation and data movement: from frameworks that enable *interactive steering and observation* of parallel codes (e.g., "Falcon: On-line monitoring and steering of large-scale parallel programs" and "Falcon—toward interactive parallel programs") to scalable data staging approaches for petascale workflows (e.g., "Datastager: scalable data staging services for petascale applications") and unified I/O frameworks for scientific data management ("ADIOS 2: The adaptable input output system"). He has developed in situ and streaming analytics approaches (e.g., "Goldrush", "Flexpath", "FlexIO", "I/O containers", "Just in time: adding value to the io pipelines...") to reduce I/O bottlenecks and enable real-time insight. Complementary lines of work include high-performance publish/subscribe and event delivery for HPC ("Publish-subscribe for high-performance computing", "Event services for high performance computing", "The ECho Event Delivery Systems"), efficient wire formats and portable binary representations ("Efficient Wire Formats for High Performance Computing", "Native data representation"), and enterprise-scale operational information flows and self-managing systems ("imanage", "Operational information systems"). More recent contributions extend these ideas to heterogeneous and accelerator-rich environments (e.g., "Lynx", "Landrush") and to transitions from file-based to streaming pipelines ("Transitioning from file-based HPC workflows to streaming data pipelines with openPMD and ADIOS2").

## Notable Works
- Falcon — "Falcon: On-line monitoring and steering of large-scale parallel programs" (1995): a foundational framework for *online monitoring and steering* of parallel applications that demonstrated how interactive control and observation can improve application performance and correctness; one of Eisenhauer’s most-cited and influential early contributions.  
- Datastager — "Datastager: scalable data staging services for petascale applications" (2009): a seminal paper addressing the I/O and noise challenges of petascale systems by introducing scalable data-staging services to reduce I/O costs and variability for checkpointing and output workflows.  
- ADIOS 2 — "Adios 2: The adaptable input output system. a framework for high-performance data management" (2020): leadership in a modern, widely adopted I/O framework that unifies APIs for scalable I/O, in situ analysis, and streaming across supercomputers, clouds, and personal systems.

## Academic Background
Greg Eisenhauer is a long-standing member of the Georgia Tech College of Computing faculty and a prolific contributor to the high-performance computing and distributed systems communities, with over 4,068 citations across his publications. His publication record (spanning the mid-1990s to the present) shows sustained leadership in middleware, runtime systems, and end-to-end data pipelines for both scientific and enterprise applications. His projects have produced widely used software (notably ADIOS2) and influenced practice at national-scale HPC centers and enterprise deployments, reflecting extensive collaborations with systems groups and domain scientists. Eisenhauer has served as principal investigator or co-investigator on multiple funded research efforts that bridge academia, national labs, and industry; he is also active in mentoring graduate students and postdoctoral researchers who advance topics in *scalable I/O*, *in situ analytics*, and *streaming data infrastructures*.
